data.title: Leggett & Platt: The Somnigroup Merger Is A Decent Exit, But No Bargain
data.description:
Leggett & Platt, Incorporated (LEG) is having a very positive day, with the stock surging 12% after the confirmation of the deal with Somnigroup International Inc. (SGI). What impressed me is that even with this important trigger, the stock price is still below what it was in my last article, that is, it really proved to be a Cigar Butt that did not offer “enough puffs.”

But what matters is that now the analysis changes completely. We are no longer talking about Leggett & Platt as a standalone company, but rather about the merger that should happen in the next quarters. What we really need to analyze, in fact, is the final company of this M&A, considering that now Somnigroup and Leggett finally “shook hands.”

A Word About the Deal

Detailing a bit more, the all-stock transaction is valued at $2.5 billion. The ratio is ~0.145 SGI shares for each LEG share. That is why Leggett is being traded for about $11.15 now, a small discount over the value of ~$11.50 that is found through the SGI stock that is at $79.1.

The expectation is that the transaction should be closed until the end of this year (2026), after the necessary approvals: LEG’s shareholders and also from other regulatory bodies. Because of that, there is a slight discount - the opportunity cost until the closing of 2026, the uncertainties of this deal really occurring without any impediment, and of course, the uncertainty of the price of the SGI stock until then.

But even with this complexity, the structure and incentives of the deal are simple. The SGI shareholders will be diluted, and the shareholders of LEG after the deal is concluded will have a stake in Somnigroup. And as this was already a long-lasting partnership, more specifically, about 50 years of collaboration, the intention of the deal is also a bit obvious. Somnigroup wants to internalize this old supplier, because it thinks it is at an interesting value, and sees a lot of potential for synergies.

At first, it seems to me to be something accretive for both. The LEG shareholder now no longer has the uncertainties and high dependence that it had on a deep value case and quite risky, and the SGI shareholder now has a new trigger of efficiency to be captured in the next years.

New Company, New Moats, New Triggers

In this type of operation, maybe the Leggett & Platt shareholder may want to make a short term trade to try to capture some additional upside or downside, for instance, try to arbitrate some potential of the deal being revised upward, it is possible to draw a parallel with what happened with Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) in the last quarters. But particularly, I do not think that this is the main point of the question.

Both for the Leggett & Platt shareholder and for the SGI shareholder, I think that the ideal is to try to understand what are the new risks and new potentials of this new company, and then try to understand if it makes sense or not to continue being shareholder. E.g., if the conclusion of the shareholder is that there is still a relevant potential and a value to be captured, either through upside or shareholder yield, good, keep the shares, if you think it is something bad, exit the operation.

There are synergies to be captured, but there is also a component of moat increase for Somnigroup. This is because, for ~50 years these two companies were partners, and now Somnigroup brings all of this in-house. Now, Somnigroup will be the owner of the technology, there is an intangible gain here of being the owner of the innovation and being able to use this as a proprietary engine for other premium brands in general.

But besides this intangible gain, there are tangible gains which were what I mentioned about synergy. In the first 12 months there is already an expectation of ~$10 million of EBITDA increase for Leggett & Platt, further ahead capturing more $40 million, totaling $50 million in synergies. A part of this is sourcing and operations, improving supply integration and increasing logistics and manufacturing efficiency through scale and similar.

Another part is through innovation, which even though it is the smaller part in financial terms, it is something interesting to think about. As Somnigroup is a large company, there is an important component of expertise of engineering, design, and volume of data that Leggett & Platt will be able to use to optimize its products and serve better the final consumer.

And well, I know that $50 million seems little for a deal of this size, mainly thinking as the SGI shareholder. But we have to think that the whole operation becomes more “smooth.” There is an increase of verticalization. The friction decreases, so Somnigroup manages to control inventory issues more effectively, same for supply chain, and give a bigger match between demand and production.

So I believe that there is a stronger strategic component, more than only financial synergies. An interesting point that they raised in the presentation was the increase of the total addressable market (TAM). Somnigroup today is a 100% bedding company (looking to its product mix). The combined company will have 12% of top line coming from furniture, flooring and textile, and 10% coming from specialized products.

By the way, in the bedding market itself there is a considerable expansion. Somnigroup cites other geographies, that’s why the “geographic mix” increases in the international segment, there is also an entire consolidated operation in foam and adjustable bed, this all increases the diversification and verticalization of Somnigroup.

New Risks For The Combined Company

Ok, so until now we have that this combined company will capture some synergies, expand moat, partnerships, and addressable market. Summarizing, it will become a better, more complete company.

But it also changes the risk profile.

Somnigroup was already a case that had synergies to be captured by other acquisitions like the one of Mattress Firm, and it is something that even improves a bit the track-record of Somnigroup, it has already done large acquisitions before. But at the same time, it increases the integration risk.

M&A is always very simple on paper, but in practice it is another story.

There is always some level of uncertainty, something that was supposed to be simple execution and in fact turned out different from planned. Some contracts that would be easily revised and now there is a fine, some cost that would “disappear” because it was redundant and did not come out as the management expected, some systems that were not of easy integration and so on. Even more intangible things like culture, can put an additional challenge in this.

But for the question of Leggett & Platt there is a more specific challenge. Today, LEG is a "neutral" company, right? Just as it was an important supplier for Somnigroup, it is also the supplier of other companies. These companies are competitors of Somnigroup.

If you are a Somnigroup competitor, will you continue buying from Leggett & Platt post-merge?

I am not saying that the customers will stop buying, I am just raising an uncertainty. Somnigroup will maintain Leggett & Platt operating in a separate way and this mitigates a bit the risks, but maybe there is a more negative perception by the customers, maybe a certain resistance of some to maintain the purchase volume of a company that now was acquired by an important rival.

If this really is an uncertainty that materializes, maybe we'll have a scenario where there is a margin expansion by the captured synergies, but a lower revenue.

Bottom Line: An Optimistic Stance, But Not That Much

From a Leggett & Platt shareholder perspective, the deal seems minimally interesting to me. There is not a substantial premium, the acquisition multiple is still small, but at least it is a decent “exit.” And for those who intend to maintain the shares of Somnigroup, it is a decent combined company.

Ex-Leggett & Platt acquisition, SGI was already an interesting company. A strong brand power, vertical integration and a lot of scale. With the acquisition of LEG, this only increases. One more company in the portfolio, more technology, more scale, more margin expansion to come in the next few years.

But particularly, it is not a case that catches my attention currently. The combined company will have ~$11.2 billion of top line and ~$1.7 billion of adj. EBITDA. Today SGI is a company of $23 billion of enterprise value, and with the acquisition the enterprise value will increase some hundreds of millions with the debt of Leggett & Platt; as well as with the issuance of shares it may increase a bit more (~$2.5 billion in new shares). With the current EV, the implied EV-to-EBITDA is 13.5x, potentially higher at the end of the acquisition due to the factors mentioned.

Even with the positive prospects of margin increase and an eventual better cycle for this niche (which would increase the top line), it does not seem like a bargain to me. And yes, the expected growth is high here (see the table below). In fact, that’s another reason I’m a bit more skeptical. The market is already pricing in an EPS CAGR of +20% for the coming years, so if any uncertainties materialize and SGI fails to deliver on that, the stock could see some de-rating.

So even though Somnigroup is an interesting company and that is becoming stronger, I do not think that it is worth a buy rating here.

Because of that, my rating is a Hold for Leggett & Platt and for Somnigroup.

data.title: Prices Are Going Higher
data.description:
"Consumer prices soared in March, pushed higher by skyrocketing gasoline prices."

This quote is from Konrad Putzier in the Wall Street Journal.

Overall inflation, year-over-year, came in at 3.3 percent in March, up from 2.4 percent, year-over-year, in February.

"Core" inflation rose 2.6 percent in March, down from 2.7 percent in February.

Energy prices...up 12.5 percent, year-over-year, in March.

They were up 0.5 percent in February.

Gasoline prices rose 18.9 percent year-over-year.

Fuel oil jumped 44.2 percent.

Analysts have been saying that inflation is going to return.

Well, it looks as if inflation has returned.

Or, we all should be looking out for the reality that inflation has returned.

The thing is that Trump and his team have "messed around" with so many things, tariffs and all, that there are a lot of places that seem to be showing the outbreak of inflation once again.

But the full experience of a return to inflation will only be confirmed with a sustained rise in prices.

Mr. Putzier writes that

"If energy prices remain high, that could filter through to higher prices for food--fertilizer is made from natural gas--and other goods."

Putzier quotes Lindsey Piegza, chief economist at Stifel:

"We won't feel the bulk of that for perhaps a month or two."

And then there is the question mark about what is going to happen to monetary policy.

In the last couple of months, the Federal Reserve has moved back into a stance of quantitative easing.

Since early December 2025, the Fed has begun to add securities to its securities held outright. The increase has been pretty aggressive, especially since Trump began his little "battle" with Iran. The "war" began on February 28, 2026, and more purchases followed. This is the result that the President seemed to want.

It seems that the stock market has gotten with these moves and started to move up again in the past week.

This is a needed component of the future health of consumer spending and economic growth.

Lydia DePillis, writing in the New York Times, emphasizes this reliance upon "wealth effects" driving greater consumer spending...which results in faster economic growth.

DePillis explains how the wealth effect works. It comes from "the free-spending mentality among higher-income earners fostered by the ballooning value of investment portfolios and retirement accounts in recent years."

The wealth generated by higher stock prices means greater consumer wealth, and this leads to greater spending on consumer goods and services...followed by..."overall" GDP growth!

This has been the picture of "how things work" that is built into the Federal Reserve's picture of how monetary policy can guide the economy.

And, for the past seventeen years or so...the Federal Reserve seems to have been very successful in seeing this narrative work out.

So, DePillis provides a picture of where the Federal Reserve appears to be going as it expands its securities portfolio.

And here is the picture of U.S. economic growth, year-over-year, going back to the Great Recession in 2009.

The point I am trying to get to is that the Federal Reserve is in a position to keep the U.S. economy expanding and expanding at a rate of growth that is in excess of 2.0 percent.

The growth rate of the M2 money stock is now around 4.4 percent, year-over-year.

The M2 velocity of circulation appears to be relatively constant over the past year or so, but here is where all of the disruptions surrounding tariffs and other policy uncertainties can cloud up the picture.

The disruptions being created will mostly impact the velocity of circulation of the money stock. So, this is where the greatest amount of "unknown" will come from.

And, in these areas, experts are expecting the most action...the most impact on the final results from the "fiscal" side of the equation.

That is, the experts are expecting that the velocity of circulation of the money stock will impact the results.

And the U.S. economy may give us an economic growth within the 2.0 percent to 2.5 percent range; the rate of inflation is expected to be closer to what was produced in March...or even higher.

The crucial thing, as Mr. Putzier points out, is that these actions need to work themselves out. This will take some time.

So, investors need to stay alert.

Everything...now...seems to be working to push inflation to a higher rate. Even the Federal Reserve seems to have moved in this direction.

And, if the Federal Reserve has moved in this direction....

data.title: Anthropic Mythos Or Not, Software And Cybersecurity Are Still Uninvevstable
data.description:
The selloff in software is a painful lesson for contrarian investors to never underestimate the power of irrational pessimism.

What selloff?

Today, software is outperforming any other industry in tech.

I'm talking about the decline since October 2025, with the iShares Expanded Tech-Software Sector ETF (IGV) and the Global X Cybersecurity ETF (BUG), down approximately 35%, mostly on what I think are irrational fears about the AI disruption in the industry.

Yes, I said the word irrational, but I have a hold rating on the IGV and BUG ETFs.

Some readers may be confused at this point. I mean, there is a clear disconnection between price action and fundamentals, right? Isn't that a mispricing opportunity that can be exploited?

All of these are valid questions. In fact, as I'm about to discuss in this piece, I strongly believe the AI disruption fear is completely irrational.

However, that doesn't mean that the lows are in. In fact, the downside catalysts after the release of a new LLM model, like the Mythos this week, make the long-term setup very fragile.

Let me be crystal clear: AI is not removing the real bottleneck in software, in my view. Building the product is only step zero. The hard part is distribution, user acquisition, and access to capital and an audience. AI is not useful for the latter as much as it is for writing code.

On top of that, the Mythos is likely going to drive up the cybersecurity budget of large corporations in the U.S., which is a tailwind for cybersecurity stocks.

However, regardless of whether the disruption thesis is true in practice or not, perception drives price more than operating reality (i.e., fundamentals). At this point, swimming against the current makes little sense to me. That's why I'm staying on the sidelines.

A Fragile Setup

In my view, this industry is the most fragile theme to own in one's portfolio, even beyond private credit.

It has nothing — let me be crystal clear — absolutely nothing to do with fundamentals or any other rational argument.

As I wrote many times before, the AI disruption fear appears irrational to anyone with hands-on experience in the software space.

How would I know?

Well, I own Guidance Terminal. If you've ever visited my site, how many visits do you think I get each day?

  • 1,000?
  • 10,000?
  • 100,000?

Please take a look at the analytics page showing the visits in the last 7 days.

I hope I'm making myself very clear: having a product in hand and being successful "ain't the same ting", as Fat Tony (from Taleb's Antifragile book) would say.

In the last 5-6 years, I developed many software tools in many niches, and the results I got were similar to the one above.

Why?

Because having the software in hand is just step 0.

The real work begins with the user acquisition phase. That's when founders need deep pockets (most of the time from naive VCs or private capital firms) and key connections with those who own the audience.

Guess what? AI is not going to help these founders with these two items, and user acquisition is, by far, the most important part of the software business journey.

Add on top of that obstacles like the headline below, and the vibecoding crowd has a slim chance (at best) to displace the incumbents.

Bottom line: videcoding as a tool, even when orders of magnitude superior to the incumbents, is far (and I really mean far) from taking their pie.

That's just my first argument on why the AI disruption fear is irrational.

So, why were software and IT services down in the last week, when every other industry in tech is in the green after the ceasefire in the Middle East?

Simply, because investors ain't developers or software founders with hands-on experience. That's why headlines like the one below make them panic sell first, and ask questions (if any) later.

The reason I say software is fragile is that as soon as the big two LLM startups, namely OpenAI (OPENAI) and Anthropic (ANTHRO) (sorry, Gemini and Grok), release a new model, the stocks in software take an instant hit.

Therefore, the amount of downside catalysts ahead is significant, and given the fact that each LLM model is better than the last, each hit on the IGV (software ETF) may be harder than the last.

Anthropic's Mythos Model

This week, the main headline in the AI space was the release (not to the general public, though) of the Claude Mythos Preview model. Specifically, the company introduced Project Glasswing. I include an interesting excerpt below from the press release:

We're partnering with the organizations responsible for the infrastructure billions of people depend on, and giving their defenders a head start with our newest frontier model, Claude Mythos Preview. The initiative brings together Amazon Web Services, Anthropic, Apple, Broadcom, Cisco, CrowdStrike, Google, JPMorganChase, the Linux Foundation, Microsoft, NVIDIA, and Palo Alto Networks as launch partners.

According to Anthropic, the Mythos model has already identified thousands of zero-day vulnerabilities across critical infrastructure. Naturally, this release brought government attention to this tool.

According to some sources, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent and Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell convened an urgent meeting with major bank CEOs to warn them about cyber risks posed by Mythos.

Naturally, this led to another selloff in software and cybersecurity stocks while the broader tech sector remained intact.

Therefore, the note that Wedbush recently published on Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR) after the software selloff may be completely accurate (in my view), but it's missing the point that the market doesn't care if "Anthropic is eating Palantir's lunch" is a fictional narrative or not.

We acknowledge the growth from Anthropic is unprecedented, but we believe the take that Anthropic is eating PLTR's lunch (most recently amplified by Michael Burry's now-deleted post on X earlier today) is the wrong take and fictional narrative (in our view), as Palantir is at the epicenter of leaders in the AI Revolution while its AIP product moat remains unmatched in our view," Wedbush noted. "The data-driven moat by Palantir is around the data and its ontology…this is NOT being disrupted by Claude…if anything, it's accelerated on the enterprise.

The IGV and BUG ETFs

These are my two favourite ETFs to track the software and cybersecurity industries.

Let's start with the IGV. This is commonly known as the iShares Expanded Tech-Software Sector ETF, or software ETF. It's not the only one, but it's the one that is referred to most of the time when talking about software.

This ETF provides a targeted exposure to North American software companies, with a small slice of interactive home entertainment and interactive media names:

Its expense ratio of 0.39% is quite attractive, considering that the fund had 112 holdings as of April 10.

As of the latest update, the ETF had $10.13 billion in net assets and an average 30-day volume of 21.9 million at a spread of 0.01%. I include below the top 10 names, which altogether make up 62% of the portfolio.

This is not your everyday income passive fund. The IGV is a pure growth ETF, with a 3-year beta of 1.23. Naturally, one cannot expect a cheap P/E multiple given that it mainly tracks growth stocks. As of April 10, the average P/E ratio is 30.8x, and the average P/B is 5.6x.

From a pure price action perspective, this ETF is underwater in terms of 1-year returns:

That's why it's attractive to a decent amount of contrarians, based on the Seeking Alpha articles (and comments) page on this ETF in the last few months.

Moving on to the BUG ETF (as an amateur dev, I really love the ticker). The official name of this ETF is the Global X Cybersecurity ETF. This is another thematic passive ETF that aims to track the broader Indxx Cybersecurity Index.

Given the overlap that some cybersecurity companies have with software, the index requires a company to generate at least 50% of its revenue from cybersecurity activities to qualify as a buying candidate.

In terms of constituents, it's smaller than the IGV, with only 30 stocks and about $770 million in net assets. I include below the top-10 holdings, which add up to 63.52% of the fund:

Speaking of the constituents, 79% of them are based in the U.S., followed by Israel at 8.6% and Japan at 5.8%.

From an expense perspective, this fund has a 0.50% fee, higher than the IGV ETF.

What I found interesting from a price action perspective is the beta between the two ETFs.

Even though software and cybersecurity aren't the same thing, it appears that the stocks within these two industries have an apparent correlation. That's why, if I am neutral on software, I am therefore neutral on cybersecurity as well.

Looking Ahead

Based on the arguments provided above, I remain neutral on the software and cybersecurity industry as I simply don't see why any of the constituents in this pocket of the market will turn around the pessimism that surrounds their stocks.

As I argued in the last earnings season, fundamentals and growth are not enough for a turnaround in software. Therefore, I prefer to take the current pessimism seriously (despite the fact that it's grounded in an unrealistic pillar) and allocate my capital elsewhere.

data.title: Best 5 Small-Cap Stocks With Forward EPS Growth Above 125%
data.description:
Small Caps Outperform, Positioned for AI-Productivity Gains

Small-cap stocks have continued to outperform the broader market despite geopolitical uncertainty, inflation concerns, and shifting interest rate expectations. Although markets saw short-term relief following an Iran war ceasefire, volatility returned after negotiations between Tehran and the Trump administration collapsed and the U.S. threatened to impose a blockade. Still, the fundamental case for small caps remains intact, supported by slightly softer-than-expected inflation data while investors continue to rotate away from mega caps.

iShares Russell 2000 ETF (IWM) vs. S&P 500: 1Y Price Return

Moreover, many small-cap companies could become key beneficiaries of the next phase of AI adoption. We launched the PRO Quant Portfolio in June – a weekly rebalanced selection of our Top 30 Quant Strong Buy stocks spanning multiple regions and market caps, with multiple names capitalizing on high growth in AI and semiconductors. According to Wells Fargo Equity Research, AI has “evidently” boosted productivity across a wide range of industries, with revenue per worker for S&P 500 companies rising 8.1% since 2022.

As benefits from AI-driven efficiencies broaden across the economy, smaller firms appear well positioned to capture a disproportionate share of productivity gains. Wells Fargo estimates that each 1% reduction in labor costs could translate into a 2% earnings increase for the S&P 500 and as much as 6% upside for Russell 2000 companies.

Small-cap stocks come with unique risks as many tend to be more volatile than the broader market, particularly in response to macroeconomic developments such as inflation, interest rate expectations, and other factors beyond company control. This makes it especially important for investors to identify small-cap stocks with strong fundamentals using quantitative tools.

How I Found Top Small-Cap Stocks With Strong Forward EPS Growth

Using Seeking Alpha’s Stock Screener, I filtered for small-cap stocks with Strong Buy and Buy Quant Ratings and strong forward EPS growth. The five small-cap stocks in my basket span the healthcare, tech, and material sectors with and have forward EPS growth rates ranging from ~126% to more than 230%.

Seeking Alpha’s Quant Ratings are generated by a proprietary model that analyzes more than 100 metrics for each stock relative to sector peers and assigns grades across five factors: Growth, Value, Profitability, Revisions, and Momentum.

In the section below, I highlight context, recent developments, and key metrics supporting each stock’s Quant Rating and strong growth factor grades.

1. Ironwood Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (IRWD)

  • Market Capitalization: $591.90M

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Healthcare

  • Industry: Biotechnology

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/13/2026): 1 out of 951

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/13/2026): 1 out of 459

  • EPS Growth (FWD): 231.46%

Ironwood Pharmaceuticals is a Boston-based gastrointestinal drug developer. The company has bullish upward earnings revisions because demand for its flagship therapy, LINZESS, is growing. Ironwood is also advancing late-stage pipeline candidate Apraglutide. Like several buy-the-dip opportunities, the stock dipped in the last 30 days, in line with sector weakness. The stock is now trading at a significant discount to healthcare peers.

IRWD vs. iShares Biotechnology ETF (IBB): 1Y Price Return

LINZESS net sales grew 11% YoY to $865M in FY 2025 and achieved a 45% market share among adult patients with IBS-C. In Q4, LINZESS expanded its market opportunity by winning FDA approval to treat patients aged seveb to 17. Product net sales are expected to top $1.12B in FY 2026, driven by improved net pricing and demand growth.

Ironwood showcases exceptional operating income and leveraged free cash margins relative to the sector, underpinning an A+ Profitability Grade. Solid financial results and projections drive a forward EPS growth of more than 230% vs. the sector’s near 10% for an A+ Growth Grade, supported by surging cash flow and operating profit.

Ironwood is trading at 2.7x forward earnings vs. the sector’s 17x, while its trailing PEG ratio represents a 98% discount to the sector. Robust margins and earnings visibility make Ironwood my top small-cap stock with strong forward EPS growth.

2. Ichor Holdings, Ltd. (ICHR)

  • Market Capitalization: $2.00B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Semiconductor Materials & Equipment

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/13/2026): 5 out of 531

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/13/2026): 1 out of 32

  • EPS Growth (FWD): 130.29%

A provider of fluid delivery subsystems and components to major companies in the semiconductor equipment industry, Ichor Holdings has expanded its addressable market with non-chip customers like SpaceX.

Shares rose last month after Stifel upgraded the stock over the potential to expand gross margins amid improving business conditions.

ICHR vs. Semiconductor Industry Peers (XSD): 1Y Price Return

Ichor surged after delivering better-than-expected Q4 2025 results and a strong outlook based on strengthening demand, gross margin improvement initiatives, and capacity expansions in Mexico and Malaysia.

The results support strong forward EPS growth, driving an A+ factor grade. Robust estimates are backed by seven upward earnings revisions in the last 90 days, with no downward revisions. Ichor also showcases strong forward growth in EBITDA, operating profit, cash flow, and return on equity.

Ichor has a forward PEG at a 46% discount to the sector. Expanding its strategic foothold among aerospace customers, Ichor is an attractive small-cap option.

3. Compass Minerals International, Inc. (CMP)

  • Market Capitalization: $1.04B

  • Quant Rating: Buy

  • Sector: Materials

  • Industry: Diversified Metals and Mining

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/13/2026): 51 out of 281

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/13/2026): 10 out of 62

  • EPS Growth (FWD): 133.42%

Compass Minerals is a leader in the North American salt market, owning the world’s largest underground salt mine based in Goderich, Ontario. Headquartered in Kansas with facilities across the U.S., Canada, and the UK, Compass has large-scale production facilities and an extensive distribution network.

CMP vs. S&P 500 Materials Sector (XLB): 1Y Price Return

In Q1 2026, Compass reported positive quarterly net income for the first time since 2023 and increased EBITDA by more than 100%. The results were largely driven by higher pricing and expanded margins from cost reductions. Compass has significantly outperformed the sector in the past year in growth in earnings, operating profit, EBITDA, and cash flow.

Despite soaring momentum in the past year, the stock’s forward price/sales and price/cash flow multiples represent solid discounts to the sector.

4. Cognyte Software Ltd. (CGNT)

  • Market Capitalization: $592.67M

  • Quant Rating: Buy

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Application Software

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/13/2026): 48 out of 531

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/13/2026): 8 out of 175

  • EPS Growth (FWD): 133.11%

Cognyte has been among the stocks hit hard in the AI scare trade, including after Anthropic (ANTHRO) unveiled Claude Mythos, a model capable of identifying hidden cybersecurity issues. The dip offset momentum from strong earnings results and a $5M law enforcement contract to deploy the company’s tactical SIGINT solution.

The recent pullback offers a buy-the-dip opportunity, as the company showcases solid fundamentals while trading at 18x earnings – a discount to the sector of over 18%.

CGNT vs. First Trust Nasdaq Cybersecurity ETF (CIBR): 1Y Price Return

Expanded deployments and upgrades for AI-driven threat detection across military intelligence agencies, including within NATO countries, helped drive annual revenue up by double digits in FY 2026. The company also strengthened its North American presence through a partnership with a major U.S. government IT contractor.

Management said CGNT solutions have become deeply entrenched as customers deploy systems in real-world operational environments. Strong growth in EBITDA and operating profit, along with robust forward EPS growth, supports an ‘A’ factor grade. Bullish revision trends drive high earnings expectations over the next two years.

CGNT is trading at 17x forward earnings, a 20% discount to the sector, while showing attractive forward EV/EBITDA and price/sales multiples.

5. Aveanna Healthcare Holdings Inc. (AVAH)

  • Market Capitalization: $1.35B

  • Quant Rating: Buy

  • Sector: Healthcare

  • Industry: Health Care Services

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/13/2026): 72 out of 951

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/13/2026): 7 out of 48

  • EPS Growth (FWD): 126.66%

Aveanna is a leading provider of home-based healthcare services across 38 states along the East Coast and Southeast, outperforming the sector in the past year, riding strong earnings results and analyst upgrades amid resilient demand. Aveanna focuses on pediatric and complex home nursing services, benefiting from extended care needs as governments and managed care organizations increasingly look to expand capacity and lower overall treatment costs.

AVAH vs. S&P 500 Health Care Sector (XLV)

AVAH delivered its eighth straight double earnings beat in Q4 2025, driven by strong performance across private duty nursing and pediatric therapy services. Enhanced rates and volume growth lifted revenue by 27% YoY to $662M, while cost savings initiatives helped push adjusted EBITDA up 54% to $85M.

In March, AVAH expanded its geographic reach westward by acquiring Family First Holding, LLC, a pediatric home care provider with 27 locations in seven states, including Illinois, Iowa, South Dakota, and Texas. The outstanding earnings results contributed to Aveanna’s robust quant metrics, supporting an exceptional Growth Grade, while strong ROE and return on capital drive an A+ Profitability Grade.

AVAH offers a solid buying opportunity after pulling back over the past month, with the stock trading at 9.9x forward earnings vs. the sector’s 17x, while forward PEG represents a 47% discount to the sector. AVAH wraps up my basket of small-cap picks with strong forward EPS growth.

Top Small-Cap Stocks With Strong Forward Earnings Growth

Small caps have outperformed the market despite volatility fueled by the conflict in the Middle East, shifting interest rate expectations, and concerns over sticky inflation. Small-cap stocks tend to be more volatile than the market and can fluctuate based on shifting macroeconomic data and headlines. data.title: Sandisk: 3 Reasons To Sell (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Sandisk (SNDK) has almost 20x’d since it was spun out of Western Digital in February 2025 at around $40 a share.

I laid out a path to $1,000 in my last article, and we are fast approaching that target.

However, as the stock keeps climbing, it’s important to temper expectations and bullishness.

I’m going to highlight three reasons below to be cautious on Sandisk, even though I am still long

NAND cyclicality, the failure of HBF, and the already stretched valuation and technicals are all clear risk factors.

The stock now sits just shy of my PT, so I'm downgrading to Hold.

Quality, Not Quantity

Everyone understands that the demand for memory has traditionally been cyclical. The big question now, though, is whether AI has turned this into a secular growth story.

If we look at HBW, for example, we are seeing a big growth in demand, while the key players of this oligopolistic market are not increasing supply at a fast rate.

Similarly, NAND prices have skyrocketed in the last few months, and the supply constraint story is even more compelling here.

When we look at key players like Samsung and SK Hynix, they aren’t looking to expand NAND production and are instead just now focusing more on HBM.

But there's another supply threat that isn't so obvious, and that’s process node upgrades.

In other words, not more NAND, but better NAND.

This is something that the industry is pursuing and could have real implications on pricing. Better NAND could actually end up meaning less need for NAND.

We must first understand that NAND is a highly oligopolistic market. Take Samsung, Sandisk, SK hynix and Micron (MU), and you have over ⅔ of the market.

And what’s Samsung, the market leader, doing right now?

It is upgrading its NAND products.

Recent reports confirm that the company is trying to move from 128-layer to 236-layer NAND.

Essentially, this means it is stacking more storage layers in one place, which means more efficiency.

Same story with SK hynix:

The company announced on April 8 that it has begun supplying its latest cSSD, PQC21, – the first product featuring 321-layer QLC1 NAND flash technology.

The next-gen storage solution delivers high capacity, performance, and power efficiency, meeting the rising demand for efficient storage in AI PC environments.

Source: SK hynix Website

Could this be why these companies are now focusing more on HBM rather than NAND? Perhaps they see the writing on the wall.

NAND is undergoing an upgrade cycle, and this could severely limit pricing in the coming months and years, unless AI demand really expands.

HBF Is Still Speculative

But the other reasons the market is so bullish on NAND are due to the expectation that it could replace HBM.

I talked about this a bit in my first Sandisk article.

A lot of the companies mentioned above are now working on High-bandwidth Flash, HBF, which could potentially also really increase demand.

In theory, NAND and DRAM complement each other, but there’s an idea that HBF could now even displace HBM.

The idea behind HBF is simple. Stack multiple NAND chips vertically, like HBM does with DRAM.

Technically, this could produce speeds and higher capacity comparable to HBM, at a fraction of the cost. Some even believe it could overtake HBM by 2038.

Sandisk and SK hynix are working on HBF products that could hit the market by 2027.

But there are two issues with this thesis.

First, the technology may not work. NAND was designed for storage, not active memory, and it has fundamental limitations that don't disappear just because you stack it cleverly.

Then there's also the commercial and implementation risk.

This new technology would have to be adopted by hyperscalers, which will take time and testing.

And again, even if this is a success, there will be an onslaught of companies rushing to implement this solution, which will dilute earnings.

Valuation And Technicals Stretched

With Sandisk now at over $950, much of the good news is priced in, but not the downside.

In my last piece, I laid out a price target of $1000, based on earnings estimates and the PEG ratio.

But now the valuation is beginning to catch up to expectations.

Current earnings and revenue estimates have Sandisk peaking in 2027.

Yes, the forward PE looks attractive now, but this will change quickly if earnings peak, and this can happen in many ways, as discussed above. It could be an end to the memory cycle, but also increased NAND efficiency and competition.

Sandisk now trades at almost 15x forward EV/EBIT. That’s already within fair value territory.

And then there are the technicals.

We're overbought on the weekly and showing a bearish divergence. This could extend further, but the risk of a technical pull-back is also rising significantly.

I'm downgrading to Hold

Despite all of this, I remain long Sandisk.

While process node improvements could be a headwind for NAND prices, the reality is that most of the capital is still being diverted toward HBM, and that keeps NAND structurally tighter than in previous cycles.

Second, demand is not going away. AI inference requires massive amounts of memory, and there’s just not enough to meet the market's expectations right now.

And third, there is still optionality.

HBF may fail, or it may take longer than expected, but if it works even partially, it changes how NAND is perceived.

More importantly, it potentially drastically increases the company’s TAM.

So while I acknowledge the risks, I am not calling the top yet.

Final Thoughts

Sandisk is no longer an undiscovered opportunity, but it sits in a tightening supply environment, with real demand tailwinds and the potential to tap into a whole new market, HBF.

However, this upside is now well known, and investors should consider the many risks.

I am downgrading to a Hold and would even downgrade to a Sell if we kept rallying another 15% or so.

data.title: Tesla's Deliveries Reinforce How It Has Further To Decline
data.description:
Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) is down by double-digits, almost 9% more than the S&P 500 (SPY), since our last article rating it as a Strong Sell. The article discussed how Tesla couldn't hide weakness forever, and with the company's deliveries report, the weakness is out. Combined with concerning results around battery storage, and a SpaceX IPO (SPACE), and Tesla remains a Strong Sell.

Tesla Deliveries and Storage

Tesla had more than 408K vehicles in production in the quarter, the vast majority of which were the Model 3/Y.

Out of this, the company delivered a measly just over 358K vehicles, with just under 342K vehicles from the Model 3/Y. There's some concerns here. First, the other models includes what's supposed to be exciting new vehicles such as Tesla's Cybertruck. Once over 1 million people wanted a Cybertruck. They never showed up.

The higher deliveries versus production for this other models segment shows that production is ramping down. For perspective, this is better than Q1 2025, but the company's 2nd weakest quarter of the last 5. The concern here is a 50k production delivery gap versus 36k a year ago, implying that the company's delivery demand is weak.

Another concern here is the deployment of only 8.8 GWh of energy storage products, as seen in the company's press release above. A year ago that number was 10.4 GWh, and that was the company's weakest quarter for energy storage of the past 5. Energy storage is supposed to be the major growth segment for Tesla, so a decline for the quarter implies this growth segment might not be what was once promised.

The segment reported 29% YoY growth last quarter, that's now a more than 15% decline.

Tesla Financial Forecast

Financially, Tesla is likely to have an incredibly weak quarter off of these reported results.

A year ago the company saw $664 million in FCF. A 6% improvement in delivered vehicles means ~$870 million in additional automotive revenue assuming the same ASP. However, the decline in storage sales means more than ~$400 million in revenue decline proportionally. That means ~$470 million in revenue growth.

Assuming margins remain equally strong, and capex remains constant, that's a $60 million FCF improvement. Versus the $664 million FCF number a year ago and that implies $725 million in FCF for the quarter (<$3 billion annualized). Sure Tesla is profitable, but this is also a $1.1 trillion company.

Elon Musk set a 50% annualized growth rate goal in 2021, but the company has never hit that goal, with vehicle sales lower in 2025 than 2023. The company's results show no major return to growth and we expect its financials to remain weak. That keeps Tesla's fair valuation at a fraction of what we forecast, in our view.

SpaceX IPO Concern

The imminent IPO of SpaceX (SPACE) at a $1.75 trillion valuation is a major concern, in our view.

Elon Musk owns ~42% of SpaceX with ~79% voting share through a dual-class ownership setup. It's clear that SpaceX is Elon Musk's preferred vehicle given his ownership stake. Elon Musk has stated before he would need substantially more ownership in Tesla to continue its work on AI and robotics, and he recently had SpaceX take over X (Twitter) + xAI (X.AI).

Now with SpaceX's IPO expected to be the largest ever, raising $75 billion at this $1.75 trillion, and he now has a new public vehicle for his goals with a much more substantial voting share. Tesla has invested $2 billion in xAI, and we foresee a world where Tesla simply reduces to its (minimal cash flow) as what it has to offer, in terms of its worth to Elon Musk.

With the majority of Elon Musk's net worth now in SpaceX, reporting is that his focus is moving away too. That could be a big impact for Tesla's premium valuation versus other car makers.

Iran-U.S.-Israel Conflict and Oil Prices

There's some talk that the current war and high oil prices could boost EV demand. This is already a discussion being made.

By buying an electric vehicle “You can save real money while doing a small part to help stabilize the climate, defund Big Oil and even reduce the risk of future conflicts in fossil-fueled nations like Iran and Venezuela.” Opinion | Now Is the Perfect Time to Buy an Electric Vehicle - The New York Times.

However, in 2008, at a time of record higher oil prices, light trucks (with less MPG) saw demand decline more aggressively than passenger cars. However, that rebound rapidly changed and SUVs continued to expand substantially. As a result, we don't expect the current conflict to dramatically change the demand for EVs here solely based on prices.

Tesla Valuation

Putting together a fair market value valuation for Tesla and we have massive concern over the company's share price.

Market Valuation Source
Vehicle Sale $58 billion Mercedes / BMW vehicle sales as a premium vehicle.
Energy Storage $8 billion LG Energy Solution's $60 billion market cap and 300 GWh annual production versus Tesla's 40 GWh.
FSD $126 billion Waymo which has dramatically more FSD miles, though if Tesla can achieve FSD it can scale it faster.
Optimus $39 billion Valuation of Figure AI, the leading humanoid robot provider in the field.
Total $231 billion 79% decline from present share price

The above valuation forecast shows a 79% decline from the present day share price, an enormous decline. That's after an almost 20% decline in Tesla's share price YTD. It's worth noting that this is an incredibly optimistic valuation in our view, for example, more than half of the valuation is FSD but Tesla still has follow cars in Austin versus fully autonomous rides like Waymo.

Alphabet's (GOOG) Waymo has 500K rides per week. That's 26 million driverless rides a year. Even if Tesla can scale FSD the first mover margins are gone. Regardless this downside, supported by not just vehicle numbers but a very concerning decline in the storage business, shows Tesla's overly high valuation.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is government market interference. Elon Musk clearly had a strong position with the White House at one point, reaching a point where President Trump advertised Teslas on the White House lawn. Government EV subsidies have benefited Toyota substantially. Government support for EVs, potentially to avoid revenue for Iran and Russia, could substantially support Tesla.

Conclusion

Tesla recently revealed its deliveries and energy storage numbers. There's a major concern there, not in the deliveries which remain weak, but in the fact that the company's energy storage declined for the first time in a number of quarters. That shows the impact of major competition that Tesla faces in every segment of its market.

At the same time, SpaceX will IPO soon, giving Elon Musk access to a vehicle he has substantially more say over, and now one that will be worth more with more cash. That could lead to investors moving away from Tesla, moving it down towards fair value. That reaffirms Tesla, despite its YTD weakness, as a strong Sell in our view.

data.title: Franklin Resources: March AUM Data Is A Potential Warning Sign
data.description:
Franklin Resources (NYSE:BEN), and its associated Franklin Templeton brand, are a household name in the investment landscape. The company has been in business for nearly 80 years now and is known as a leader and innovator in the fund management space.

And, with 46 consecutive years of dividend increases, income investors have come to trust in BEN stock as a stable pick to own for the long-haul.

Previously, Franklin Resources had been more of a growth story, with solid earnings per growth increases which led to a more generous valuation multiple. Since about 2015, however, EPS growth stalled out and the firm's P/E ratio has dipped. In conjunction with that, Franklin Resource's dividend yield has now surged to more than 5%:

At first glance, this might seem like quite the attractive opportunity for income investors with the more than 5% dividend from a well-known Dividend Aristocrat trading at a modest starting valuation.

But, I don't think it's quite that simple. I've been a skeptic about various traditional asset managers, including Franklin Resources, as potential investment opportunities. And that remains the case today. Here's why.

Assets Under Management Starts To Wobble

Franklin Resources had been able to point to a slight improvement in its assets under management ("AUM") trend over the past year. And that's likely been what's driven the rebound in the firm's share price since the 2025 lows:

However, as I noted in my prior article on the company, investors shouldn't mistake a bull market for an improved business.

Because, at the end of the day, asset growth can either be driven by inflows, or by changes in the value of investments that are currently being managed. A rising market can obscure weakness with a fund's marketing or performance for a time. But when stock prices turn lower, that puts to the test how sticky the firm's existing assets are, and also how willing clients are to put more money in on dips. Much of the fund management business relies on inertia, and half the battle is collecting money in the first place. But, in times of volatility, people rethink their capital allocation and so formerly stable pools of money can start to move about more quickly.

As geopolitical concerns picked up in March, that started to have a negative impact on sentiment. And, with Franklin's recent March assets under management press release, we can now quantify that impact. Here is Franklin's preliminary AUM table for the period ending March 31, 2026:

At first glance, the AUM picture doesn't look all that concerning. Sure, total AUM was down more than 3% in March compared to the prior month. But, the decline was less than 1% on a year-to-date-basis. And total AUM is still up nearly 10% versus March of 2025. Not too shabby.

But I want to highlight a couple of things from the above table. First, look at the equity AUM line in particular. Equity AUM fell by 7% in March versus the close of business in February. That outpaced the roughly 5% decline in the major market indexes for that month. And the equity AUM figure at the end of March came in well below the end of Q3 and end of Q4 2025 figures as well.

Recall that Franklin's equity AUM had jumped from $598 million in March 2025 to as high as $722 million at the end of February 2026. In isolation, that could seem like a sign of overall stabilization in the business and proof of concept that BEN stock was being overly discounted in the marketplace. However, with March's market sell-off, Franklin's equity AUM gave back much of the prior year's gains, and the drop was steeper than that of the overall stock market.

I'm not especially worried that the Iran crisis is going to turn into a full-blown bear market. This probably isn't the start of the "big one", so to speak. But there will inevitably be another major bear market at some point for one reason or another.

To be fair, Franklin manages more than just equity funds. Arguably the most strength has been found in multi-asset, which notched a slightly higher AUM figure for March as compared to February and has posted a double-digit year-over-year jump. The alternative AUM category also continued to grow in March. These are encouraging results, especially as I'd expect there to be less fee erosion pressure on these categories as compared to equities where low-cost ETFs continue to greatly impact profit margins for traditional fund managers. But these categories remain a fairly modest chunk of Franklin's overall AUM.

On the fixed income side, Franklin is still struggling to bounce back from its issues with that category over the past couple of years. Franklin's fixed income AUM fell by a couple of percent in March and is down by a similar amount on a rolling 12-month basis. Traditionally, fixed income tends to benefit from inflows when investors become more nervous about stock market performance, but that has come into question a bit over the past few years as bonds have struggled in the more inflationary environment.

Franklin Makes Another Move In Crypto

Also in the news, earlier in April, Franklin Resources announced that it is acquiring 250 Digital, which it describes as follows:

"[250 Digital is] an active cryptocurrency investment management firm led by financial industry veterans Christopher Perkins and Seth Ginns of CoinFund Management LLC. The acquisition would include the 250 Digital investment team and all liquid cryptocurrency strategies previously run by CoinFund. Franklin Templeton will invest in the strategies as part of the agreement."

CoinFund does not appear to have an especially wide online footprint, and I couldn't find many details about AUM or other specifics for this deal. Franklin noted that this will become part of a new effort, Franklin Crypto, which consolidates this acquisition with the existing Franklin Templeton Digital Assets operation. The digital assets arm had $1.8 billion in AUM as of year-end 2025, which was well under 1% of the firm's total AUM.

I'm not against innovation and engaging in M&A with less traditional asset managers. That said, it feels like the hot moment for more specialized crypto concepts such as non-fungible tokens, staking, metaverse-based land, blockchain innovations, etc. has largely passed. In other words, we aren't in 2022 anymore. There's still some significant investment demand for the most well-known cryptocurrencies like Bitcoin (BTC-USD). But I'm less convinced that these sorts of broader cryptocurrency ecosystem plays have all that much upside in the current market environment.

BEN Stock Verdict

I've previously argued that I see Franklin Resources as a potential "value trap" investment. I am sticking with that opinion today, especially in light of March's AUM data.

If the overall U.S. stock market resumes its healthy climb that we've seen in recent years, Franklin Resources can make the most of its existing asset base. In that scenario, the stock is priced cheaply enough that investors could make a reasonably strong return going forward. That's certainly a possibility.

That said, U.S. equity markets have been on a fairly steady upward trajectory since 2023. In my opinion, the longer things have been stable, the more that leads to volatility once the market structure changes. Again, I'm not losing sleep over the Iran crisis in particular. But, the recent market dip serves as a wake-up call as to how quickly Franklin Resources' equity AUM can and likely would erode in a real and prolonged market downturn.

While BEN stock is down fractionally since my prior coverage of the company, shares are still up close to 40% over the past 12 months. In my view, that makes for a great exit point.

Because, when that next bear market does come, we'll probably see a significant drop in AUM, fall in earnings per share, and a potential dividend cut all rolled into one. While that outcome may still be a few years off, I see it as having a high probability of eventually occurring. Thus, income investors may be on borrowed time sticking with BEN stock as a steady dividend option.

data.title: Capital One: A Payments Platform Hiding Inside A Bank Stock
data.description:
Capital One Financial (COF) is a mispriced large cap U.S. bank stock which trades relatively cheaply at ~1.8x P/TB on 15.8% FY26E RoTCE as it has underperformed in the past year with a 20.7% return compared with the S&P Bank index's (KBE) 39% growth. Most of the softness occurred in early 2026, as the market parsed through the headwinds it had been facing—a statutory 10% credit rate cap, credit quality deterioration, and a more costly Discover integration. But, we believe the market is not fully appreciating the structural transformation that the company has underwent to convert itself from a monoline card issuer into a vertically integrated payments and lending platform, which is unique among the top five U.S. card issuers.

Acquiring Discover for transformation

Capital One completed the acquisition for Discover Financial Services in May 2025 for $51.8B, marking one of the largest U.S. bank deal in over ten years. Through this acquisition, COF bought $168.6B in identifiable assets, including $108.2B in loans and $106.9B in deposits (FY25 annual report, pp.5). In addition, the acquisition brought in the Discover payment network, the PULSE debit network and the Diners Club International network.

Not only just a balance sheet acquisition, the Discover deal is a structural repositioning for COF as it allowed the company to own the payment rail, whereas before, COF had to pay Visa (V) / Mastercard (MA) interchange and network fees for every card transaction.

The management has outlined $2.7B in total synergies by FY2027E which comprises ~$1.5 in expense synergies (reducing overlap in admin costs and headcount, consolidating technology costs) and ~$1.2B in network synergies (Durbin Amendment debit exemption, reduced networks fees).

The Durbin Amendment windfall arises from a regulatory arbitrage estimated to be ~$1.2B annually by FY2027E. Under U.S. law, the Federal Reserve's debit interchange fee caps only apply to four-party networks like Visa and Mastercard, whereas Discover network operates as a closed-loop network and is exempt, meaning it can charge higher transaction fees.

Financial performance

FY2025 was a year of transformation for the company as it digested the Discover acquisition. The surge in provision for credit losses was mainly attributable to the one-time, day-one non-PCD (purchased credit deterioration) reserve build for the Discover loans acquired at closing. If we exclude that, adjusted EPS would have been $19.61 in FY2025, reflecting an accelerating underlying business from FY24's $11.59.

Net Income $4,887 $4,750 $2,453
Diluted EPS (GAAP) $11.95 $11.59 $4.03

Source: Company, Himalayas Research estimates

Similarly, the company delivered a robust underlying earnings for 4Q25 while the headline numbers included one-off items that led to an earnings miss. Adjusted EPS of $3.86 in Q4 missed consensus estimate of $4.12 by $0.26, which was due to a deliberate $200M philanthropic contribution and $0.04 from a pension termination charge. Both causes were non-recurring. Instead, the underlying franchise remained resilient as domestic card NCO improved 113bps Y/Y and NIM steadied above 8%.

Domestic Card NCO Rate 4.93% 4.63% 6.06% −113bps
CET1 Ratio 14.3% 14.4% 13.5% +80bps
TBV / Share $107.72 $105.18 $106.97 +$0.75
Buybacks $2.5B (11.4M shares) $1.0B (4.6M shares) ~$161M (946K shares) —

Source: Company, Himalayas Research estimates

On the Q4 earnings call, CEO Fairbank mentioned that efficiency ratio will remain pressured as the company has been making investments and digesting the Discover and Brex acquisitions. When asked whether expenses have peaked, he responded that efficiency improvement should come from revenue growth and not cost cutting. Our read is that because efficiency ratio came in at 60% in Q4 (vs. 55.8% consensus), the market might have gotten spooked that the bank is increasingly spending more to buy growth. Given that the stock has pulled back substantially since last earnings, we believe that this is less of a concern now at current levels.

Catalysts

COF's catalysts revolves around synergy realizations with its recent acquisitions—Discover for inorganic consumer credit card revenue and NIM growth, and cost savings and Brex for commercial card market expansion. Further, improvements in credit card delinquency and the broader economy, and Fed rate cuts, should help COF's underlying business. However, the 10% credit card rate cap remains an overhang for the stock. As of April 13, there is no binding legal mechanism and no Senate committee vote has been scheduled, so there is no viable path to enactment in the near term. We suggest that enactment would be a low-probability but high severity tail risk event and would devastate the company's revenue model, while causing many millions subprime borrowers to lose credit access, according to America's Credit Unions.

Catalyst Timeline Impact
Synergy realization milestones vs. 2027 $2.7B target Q2–Q3 2026 Positive — re-rates earnings multiple
Credit card NCO stabilization / delinquency improvement Q2 2026 Positive — removes key bear narrative
Credit rate cap legislative failure Ongoing Very positive — removes overhang
Discover network volume / acceptance gains 2026–2027 Positive — validates long-term network thesis
Brex integration progress / revenue ramp 2H 2026 Positive — confirms commercial strategy
Buyback execution at current prices Ongoing EPS accretion, capital return signal
Fed rate cuts 2026–2027 Positive — eases deposit cost pressure

Source: Company, Himalayas Research estimates

Earnings preview

Capital One reports 1Q 2026 earnings on April 21. The market expects a seasonal EPS recovery to $4.71. We think the quarter is mostly about whether credit quality normalization story is intact—domestic card NCO rate was 4.93% in 4Q25, worsening from 3Q25's 4.63% due to seasonality. Management's commentary on this figure should tell us more on the trajectory of credit quality.

Net interest margin was 8.26% last quarter, slightly down from 3Q25's 8.36% but meaningfully improved from 4Q25's 7%. We expect NIM to be inline (similar to 4Q25) in 1Q26E. Again, the management's take on provision for credit losses and credit card volume and loan growth will be key to understanding the health of the underlying business and how well the integration of Discover has underwent.

We expect some macro headwinds in Q1 2026, particularly for the credit card market as consumers face tariff uncertainty, higher inflation from fuel prices due to the U.S.-Iran conflict, and general seasonal weakness for card issuers in the first quarter.

Valuation and verdict

COF trades at ~1.8x P/TB on 15.8% forward FY26E RoTCE, sharply derating after Trump's call for one-year cap on credit card interest rates in early January. With respect to other major U.S. banks, the stock trades attractively using our P/TB vs RoTCE valuation framework below.

We opine that with a successful integration with Discover and Brex, the stock could rerate higher in terms of P/E as it transforms into more of a credit card company like V (V), Mastercard (MA) and American Express (AXP), whose forward P/Es range from 18x for AXP to 23x-25x for V / MA. This is typically ahead of the U.S. banking group, which trades at P/E of 11x-16x.

To conclude, we think COF is mispriced as the market appears to be overly concerned with the rate cap on credit cards while COF is transitioning into a higher margin closed-loop operator with its Discover integration. Even though it is difficult to predict how the legislation will ultimately end up, it is unlikely to be a near term issue.

We rate COF as a Buy on near term weakness and that it has the right catalysts to allow for a rerating.
